The engagement

What an engagement looks like.

A structured, time-bound advisory engagement — from first conversation to final walkthrough. Everything is designed to get the right analysis in front of the right people before the decisions that matter get made.

How it works

Four phases. One week.

Every engagement follows the same structure. Not because it's a template — because the structure is what makes the output reliable.

Phase 01
Kick-off

A focused session to frame the deal — the strategic rationale, the areas of concern, and the questions that most need answering. This isn't a data-gathering exercise; it's a conversation between people who understand what's at stake.

We bring an initial read of the deal context. You bring what you know — and what keeps you up at night about this one.

Our role: We lead the session and build the analytical frame the engine works from. The questions we ask here shape everything that follows.
Phase 02
Document intake & survey

Deal documents are submitted through the secure client portal — IM, CIM, financial model, management presentations, whatever is available at this stage of the process. The engine extracts and structures the analytical signals across the deal.

Where key information is absent or ambiguous, a short targeted survey is generated — typically 8–10 questions — to fill the gaps before analysis begins. We review the intake before analysis begins.

Our role: We review the document set and survey responses, and flag anything that warrants closer attention before analysis begins.
Phase 03
Analysis

The engine analyses the deal across 16 domains — strategy alignment, talent, technology, governance, synergies, and more — drawing on 147 integration patterns and 34 sourced benchmarks. Risks are classified by severity. Decisions are surfaced. Workstreams are mapped.

This is where the depth comes from. The engine does in hours what would otherwise take weeks of consulting resource — and does it with a consistency no team can match.

Our role: Active throughout. Reviews interim outputs, pressure-tests the engine's conclusions against his own read of the deal, and resolves any judgement calls before the report is drafted. The final output reflects both the analytical rigour of the engine and the practitioner judgement behind it.
Phase 04
Report & walkthrough

Two reports are produced: a board-level integration assessment and a management execution blueprint. Both are delivered through the secure client portal, with a structured walkthrough to go through the findings, answer questions, and agree next steps.

The walkthrough is not a presentation. It's a working session — we take you through what the analysis found, why it matters, and what you should do about it.

Our role: We lead the walkthrough and remain available for follow-up questions after delivery.

What you receive

Two reports. One for the board. One for the team.

Both are produced within five working days of the kick-off. Both arrive through the secure client portal, ready to use.

O1 · Board level
Integration Risk Assessment
A clear-eyed assessment of integration risk and opportunity for senior leadership — structured for the IC paper or board pack.
Integration confidence verdict with rationale
Ranked risk register — critical, high, and medium
Retention and talent windows
Key decisions required before Day 1
Synergy opportunities and risks to the model
Recommended integration phasing
O2 · Management level
Management Execution Blueprint
A detailed execution framework for the integration team — the workstreams, decisions, and structure needed to start with clarity rather than chaos.
Workstream map with priorities and owners
Phase-by-phase integration roadmap
Governance and decision-rights framework
Integration Management Office setup
Synergy tracking structure
Day 1 readiness checklist

Illustrative output · Project Falcon

This is what lands with your board.

Project Falcon is a fictional deal created specifically to demonstrate the depth and structure of an Integration Intelligence output. No real transaction, company, or client is referenced anywhere in this document.

Executive Summary
Deal Assessment Scorecard
Risk Landscape

Illustrative only — no real client information. Project Falcon and Project Aurora are fictional deals created to demonstrate the output format. Integration Intelligence treats all client work as strictly confidential and does not share, reference, or disclose any client information.

Investment

One engagement. One price.

Against the cost of getting an integration wrong — or the fees involved in any other part of a deal process — this is a straightforward decision.
